title: Automated Code Review — Review Me, Review Me Not, Review Me

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you will explore the full spectrum of automated code review on GitHub: Copilot code review, custom coding guidelines, CodeQL scanning, Copilot Autofix, secret scanning, and enterprise governance.

This is a challenge lab. You already know the basics of GitHub Copilot; now you will apply that knowledge to build review workflows and security pipelines. Follow the steps in this panel as you advance through the slides.

steps.title: Diagnose Your Review Bottleneck
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and ask it to help you think through code review pain points in a real or hypothetical project.

I have a team of 8 developers with 2 senior reviewers. We average 15 PRs per day. Help me estimate how much reviewer time is spent on mechanical checks vs. architecture feedback.

Use the response to identify which review tasks are best suited for automation.

steps.title: Quantify the Cost
steps.instruction:
Follow up with a more specific question:

If each mechanical review comment takes 3 minutes to write and our reviewers leave an average of 5 per PR, how much time per week could we save with automated review?

Think about how these numbers map to your own team. Which categories of feedback (style, bugs, security, architecture) could be automated?

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Explore Project Configuration
steps.instruction:
Open the Explorer sidebar and look for project configuration files. In a real repository, this is where you would find .github/ folders, CODEOWNERS files, and workflow configs.

Understanding the project layout helps you decide where review automation configuration should live and which paths need which reviewers.

steps.title: Plan Your Review Strategy
steps.instruction:
The slides describe two trigger modes: auto-review and on-demand. Use Copilot to help you draft a CODEOWNERS file that assigns Copilot as a reviewer.

Write a CODEOWNERS file for a project with src/api/, src/frontend/, and src/shared/ directories. Assign Copilot as a reviewer for all paths and add human team leads for their respective areas.

steps.title: Compare the Approaches
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to help you think through when each approach is appropriate:

When should a team use auto-review (Copilot assigned via CODEOWNERS) vs. on-demand review (clicking the Copilot button)? Give me a decision matrix based on team size, PR volume, and risk tolerance.

Consider which approach you would recommend for a team you work with.
steps.tip: Many teams use both: auto-review for broad coverage and on-demand for targeted analysis before requesting human reviewers.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Write Vulnerable Code on Purpose
steps.instruction:
Create a new file and intentionally write code with common vulnerabilities. Try to include at least three different issue types.

Hint: think about SQL injection, missing input validation, hardcoded credentials, and unsafe deserialization. Write 3-4 short functions that each contain a different flaw.

steps.title: Review Your Own Code
steps.instruction:
Select all the code you just wrote and open Copilot Chat. Ask Copilot to review it as if it were a pull request:

Review this code as if you were a PR reviewer. For each issue, explain the vulnerability, its severity, and provide a suggested fix.

Compare Copilot's findings against the flaws you intentionally introduced. Did it catch all of them? Did it find anything you did not expect?

steps.title: Iterate on the Fixes
steps.instruction:
Apply Copilot's suggested fixes, then ask it to review the corrected code again:

I've applied your fixes. Review the updated code and confirm whether all issues are resolved, or if any new concerns were introduced.

This review-fix-review loop mirrors how Copilot code review works on real pull requests.

steps.title: Review the Diff
steps.instruction:
Open Source Control to see the diff between your original vulnerable code and the fixed version. This is exactly what a PR reviewer would see when Copilot Autofix suggests changes.

Click on a changed file to view the side-by-side diff. Notice how the fixes are minimal and targeted — good automated fixes change only what is necessary.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Source Control: Ctrl+Shift+G

steps.title: Draft Your Team's Guidelines
steps.instruction:
Coding guidelines shape what Copilot focuses on during review. Create a new file and draft 3-5 coding guidelines for a project of your choice.

Each guideline needs a title, a description, and file path patterns. Think about the rules your team enforces inconsistently today.
steps.tip: Good guidelines are specific and actionable. "Write clean code" is too vague. "All public API functions must include JSDoc with @param and @returns tags" is enforceable.

steps.title: Test Your Guidelines
steps.instruction:
Paste your guidelines into Copilot Chat and ask it to review a code sample against them:

Here are my team's coding guidelines: [paste guidelines]. Now review this code against those guidelines and flag any violations.

Write or paste a code snippet that intentionally violates one or two of your guidelines. Verify Copilot catches the violations.

steps.title: Discover Review Commands
steps.instruction:
Open the Command Palette and type copilot review to explore available review-related commands. Notice which commands are available for triggering reviews, managing guidelines, and configuring Copilot's behavior.

These commands are how power users interact with review features without navigating menus.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel and Command Palette for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P

steps.title: Trace a Taint Path
steps.instruction:
CodeQL tracks tainted data from user input to dangerous sinks. Use Copilot to explore this concept:

Show me an example of a taint path in a Node.js Express application where user input from req.query flows through two helper functions before reaching a shell exec call. Include the source, each intermediate step, and the sink.

steps.title: Break the Taint Chain
steps.instruction:
Now challenge yourself: ask Copilot how to sanitize the tainted data at each point in the chain.

For the taint path you just showed me, what are the possible sanitization points? Show me how to break the taint chain at each level: input validation, intermediate sanitization, and sink-level protection.

Understanding taint paths helps you write code that CodeQL will not flag in the first place.
steps.tip: Defense in depth means sanitizing at multiple points. Do not rely on a single sanitization step.

steps.title: Inspect Code Problems
steps.instruction:
Open the Problems Panel to check for any diagnostics in your workspace. In a real project with CodeQL and ESLint configured, this panel surfaces security findings, type errors, and lint warnings inline.

If you see any issues listed, click one to navigate to the source. This is the same experience developers get when code scanning results are pulled into their editor.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed all panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Problems Panel: Ctrl+Shift+M

steps.title: Simulate Autofix
steps.instruction:
Create a new file and write a Python Flask route with a reflected XSS vulnerability, similar to the slide example but with a different scenario. Try a template rendering flaw or an unsafe redirect.

Do not copy the slide code directly; write your own variation.

steps.title: Generate the Fix Explanation
steps.instruction:
Select your vulnerable code and ask Copilot to act as Autofix:

Act as Copilot Autofix. Identify the security vulnerability in this code, explain what it is and why it matters, then generate a minimal fix. Include only the changes needed to resolve the vulnerability.

Compare the fix to what you would have written manually. Is it minimal and correct?

steps.title: Try Multiple Languages
steps.instruction:
Repeat the exercise in a different language. Write a similar vulnerability in Java, C#, or Go and ask Copilot to generate the Autofix-style remediation.

Notice how the fix pattern changes across languages. The vulnerability concept is the same, but the remediation tools differ.

steps.title: Check Extension Output
steps.instruction:
Open the Output Panel and select the GitHub Copilot channel from the dropdown. This panel shows real-time logs from the Copilot extension, including which prompts were sent and how the model responded.

When Autofix generates a suggestion, the extension logs reveal how the fix was derived. This is useful for debugging cases where Autofix produces an incorrect or incomplete remediation.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed all panels for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Output Panel: Ctrl+Shift+U

steps.title: Build a Scanning Workflow
steps.instruction:
Create a new file named codeql-analysis.yml and write a GitHub Actions workflow that runs CodeQL analysis. The slide shows a basic example; extend it.

Your workflow should:
• Trigger on push, PR, and a weekly schedule
• Scan at least two languages
• Use the latest CodeQL action versions

Try writing it from memory first, then check against the slide.

steps.title: Add Custom Query Packs
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot how to extend your workflow with custom CodeQL query packs:

How do I add a custom CodeQL query pack to my code scanning workflow? Show me how to include both the default queries and a custom pack from my organization.

Modify your workflow file to include the custom query configuration.

steps.title: Validate the Workflow
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to review your completed workflow for correctness:

Review this GitHub Actions workflow for code scanning. Check for missing permissions, incorrect action versions, and any configuration issues.

Fix any issues Copilot identifies. A misconfigured scanning workflow gives false confidence.
steps.tip: The permissions block is commonly misconfigured. Code scanning needs security-events: write at minimum.

steps.title: Lint the Workflow Syntax
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and inspect your workflow file:

cat codeql-analysis.yml

In a real project, you would use tools like actionlint or yamllint to catch syntax errors before committing. Malformed YAML is the most common reason scanning workflows fail silently.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed all panels for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Create a Custom Secret Pattern
steps.instruction:
Secret scanning supports custom patterns via regex. Ask Copilot to help you design one:

My organization uses internal API tokens that follow the pattern ACME-[A-Z0-9]{32}. Write a custom secret scanning pattern definition that detects these tokens, including a test string that should match and one that should not.

Think about what organization-specific secrets your team uses that the default patterns would not catch.

steps.title: Test Push Protection Awareness
steps.instruction:
Create a new file and write code that contains a fake secret (do not use real credentials):

GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678

Ask Copilot what would happen if you tried to push this file to a repository with push protection enabled. Then ask how a developer should remediate it.

Understanding the push protection workflow helps you respond quickly when it blocks a real push.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Review Copilot Settings
steps.instruction:
Open Settings and search for copilot review to explore configuration options that control how Copilot behaves during code review. Look at settings for review suggestions, auto-completions, and security scanning.

Understanding these settings helps you configure enterprise-wide review policies. Many organizations enforce specific settings via managed profiles.

steps.title: Design a Ruleset
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to help you design an organization-level ruleset that combines all the tools from this session:

Design a GitHub repository ruleset for an organization that requires: Copilot code review approval, passing CodeQL scans with no high-severity alerts, at least one human reviewer from CODEOWNERS, and signed commits. Format it as a summary table with the rule name, type, and enforcement level.

steps.title: Map to Compliance Frameworks
steps.instruction:
Follow up by mapping your ruleset to a compliance framework:

Map each rule in the ruleset to the relevant SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria or ISO 27001 controls. Show which compliance requirement each automated check satisfies.

This exercise demonstrates how automated code review translates directly to audit evidence.
steps.tip: Auditors care about evidence of consistent enforcement. Automated rulesets provide that evidence for every single merge.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed all panels for you.

For future reference:
• Settings: Ctrl+,
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! You have completed Review Me, Review Me Not, Review Me.

You explored:
• How Copilot code review catches quality and security issues on PRs
• Writing custom coding guidelines that shape automated reviews
• CodeQL taint tracking and how it identifies vulnerability paths
• Copilot Autofix for generating minimal, targeted security fixes
• Building and validating code scanning workflows
• Custom secret scanning patterns and push protection
• Enterprise governance rulesets mapped to compliance frameworks

Next steps: enable code scanning on one of your repositories and assign Copilot as a reviewer on your next PR.
